1:
The chapter opens with Saul's malice at last in the open. The grammatical-historical Ellicott traces the murderous impulse of the unhappy king through its rising stages: first it showed itself only in the paroxysms of insanity, when the half-distraught Saul would grasp and poise his heavy spear as though to hurl it at the kindly musician; then it plotted and schemed against the hated life, trying to involve the young soldier in some enterprise fraught with deadly peril; now at last he speaks openly to his heir and his counsellors of the risk incurred by suffering so dangerous a man to live. Ellicott also presses the grammar: the literal Hebrew, followed by the LXX and the Syriac, reads not that they should kill David but 'that he intended to kill David' — the naked confession of a settled purpose.

The Reformed Gill probes Saul's calculation in speaking first to Jonathan. The prince was heir to the crown, and Saul reasoned that a son whose own succession seemed endangered by David's rise would surely see where his true interest lay, listen to a father rather than a friend, and abandon his rival, yea seek his ruin. The puritan Matthew Henry marvels that the king so badly misjudged both men. Never was enemy so unreasonably cruel: Saul proclaims David an outlaw and charges all his court to kill him, unashamed though he can give no reason, and unafraid to provoke servants he himself had said all loved David (1 Sam. 18:22). Yet Henry finds even here a mercy — 'there is least danger from this undisguised malice,' for Saul did not stoop to suborn false witnesses and murder David under colour of law, as Naboth was; and because Saul assumed his son must envy David as he himself did, Providence ordered it so that the very heir he trusted became David's safety.

The Wesleyan Clarke lands the theological weight of the moment: nothing less than the especial interposition of God could have saved David's life, when every officer about the king's person and every soldier had received positive orders to dispatch him. Beneath their differing emphases — Ellicott's clinical anatomy of a hardening heart, Gill's insight into the tempter's shrewdness, Henry's astonishment at unmasked hatred — the witnesses converge on one confession: that the seed of the serpent will openly seek the life of the seed of the woman, and yet cannot touch it a moment before God permits.
2:
Jonathan's warning turns the friend into David's shield. The Reformed Gill dwells on the quality of the affection that made the warning possible: Jonathan 'delighted much in David,' loving him with a love of complacency that was constant and steadfast — and Gill sees a kind providence in it, for by this friendship David came to the knowledge of Saul's designs and could the better guard against him. The grammatical-historical Ellicott underlines that the peril Jonathan discerned was 'a very present one': a very slight expression, on the part of a powerful king, of his earnest desire to be rid of an obnoxious subject is enough to stir up unscrupulous men to commit the murder they fancy will be acceptable to their master. Clarke adds the likely mechanics of the danger — perhaps the order was to slay David the very next day, and so Jonathan charges him to be particularly on his guard at that hour and to hide himself.

Keil and Delitzsch read the whole rescue as the modesty of a son. Jonathan's plan is careful and self-effacing: David is to take heed to himself, keep out of harm's way, and abide in a secret place until morning, while Jonathan goes out to stand beside his father in the field and commune with him, promising to report whatever he learns. Here the witnesses see the marks of true friendship put to the test. Jonathan continued to delight in David though David's glory had eclipsed his own; and now, when the stream ran so strongly against his friend, he bravely appeared for him rather than shelter his own succession.

The commentators note the tension in Jonathan's counsel to hide. He does not urge flight and rebellion but a prudent concealment that buys time for reconciliation — the first of many nights David must spend keeping out of Saul's reach. Gill observes that Jonathan could not be certain some of the servants might not, out of servility to Saul or envy of David, execute the deadly orders if they lighted upon him. So the verse holds two things together that the witnesses will not let fall apart: the reality of the danger, which required real caution, and the reality of the love, which moved a prince to imperil his own standing at court for a friend the king had marked for death. A friend in need, says the old proverb the expositors love here, is a friend indeed.
3:
The third verse shows the advocate laying his plan, and the witnesses read it as a small masterpiece of faithful mediation. The Reformed Gill fixes the homely detail of the setting: Jonathan will go out in the morning, at the very hour his father was accustomed to take his morning walk, that he may find him calm and open to reason. The grammatical-historical Ellicott pictures 'the field' not as open country but as some garden or quiet place whither the king was in the habit of resorting with his friends and counsellors — a setting of privacy and ease where a son might venture the most delicate of subjects.

What strikes the commentators is the deliberate care of the arrangement. Jonathan does not confront his father in the heat of the previous night, when, as Matthew Henry suggests, Saul may have been in drink and unfit to be spoken to, but waits for morning, hoping that sleep may itself have cooled the murderous order. He stations David where he can overhear, promising to commune with Saul in David's very hearing and to tell his friend what he sees. The intercession, Henry says, was managed with a great deal of the meekness of wisdom; Jonathan showed himself faithful to his friend by speaking good of him, though he risked incurring his father's displeasure by it — a rare instance of valuable friendship.

The witnesses draw out the pattern of a true advocate. Jonathan places himself between the accused and the judge, chooses the fittest time and the fittest place, and stakes his own safety on the outcome. He asks David to trust the process and remain hidden while another pleads for him — a posture the faithful know well, waiting in a secret place while an advocate stands in the field. The verse is thin in words but rich in the theology of mediation the expositors love: that God so often delivers his servants not by open force but through the courage of a friend who will risk a father's anger to speak good of the innocent, and who reports back to the anxious heart the outcome of his pleading.
4:
Now the advocate speaks, and the witnesses gather admiringly around Jonathan's plea. The Reformed Gill summarizes its substance: Jonathan observed to Saul what a good man David was and what good things he had done, what wisdom and prudence he had shown in all his affairs, what valour and courage in every expedition, and what faithfulness and integrity toward his king and country in every instance. The wesleyan Clarke notes the settled conviction behind the words — it is evident that Jonathan was satisfied David was an innocent man, and that his father was most unjustly incensed against him.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott gives the plea its full dramatic force. The heir to the throne — the one man above all others likely to be injured by the growing popularity of David — with great power and intense earnestness represents to the king the great virtues, the unrivalled gifts, and, above all, the splendid services of the young soldier whose life Saul was so anxious to cut short. Ellicott even supplies the eloquence: 'See,' urged the pleader for his friend's life, 'on that ever memorable occasion when he fought the giant, when he aimed the pebble of the brook from his shepherd's sling, he put his life in his hand. Had he missed a hair's-breadth, the giant would have slain him, and the deliverance then wrought for Israel would never have been accomplished.'

Keil and Delitzsch frame the same appeal as a careful legal warning: 'Let not the king sin against his servant, against David; for he hath not sinned against thee, and his works are very good to thee.' The wesleyan Wesley presses what the deed cost. Jonathan spake good of David, 'which he could not do without hazard to himself'; herein he performed the duty of a true friend and of a valiant man. So the witnesses unite in holding up Jonathan as the pattern of courageous love: the man with the most to lose by David's greatness becomes the boldest witness to David's innocence, teaching that faithfulness to a friend, like faithfulness to the truth, is proved precisely when it is costly.
5:
The heart of Jonathan's argument is the risk David once ran for the very king who now hunts him. The Reformed Gill lingers on the phrase 'he did put his life in his hand': David exposed himself to the utmost danger when no one in all the camp of Israel would do the like. Keil and Delitzsch set out the full weight of the plea — David risked his life, slew the Philistine, and the LORD wrought a great salvation for all Israel; Saul himself saw it and rejoiced; wherefore, then, would he sin against innocent blood and slay David without a cause? The wesleyan Clarke admires the compression of it: the pleadings in this verse, though short, are exceedingly cogent, and the argument is such as could not be resisted.

The witnesses note how Jonathan stacks the charges against any thought of murder. First, the public good: by David's hand the LORD had wrought deliverance for the whole nation, so that to slay him would be black ingratitude to a servant, a great affront to God, and a real injury to Israel. Second, and decisively, David's innocence — his blood is innocent, and if he be slain it is without cause. The expositors observe that Jonathan appeals to Saul's own memory: 'thou sawest it, and didst rejoice.' The king cannot deny the very victory over Goliath in which, as Henry notes, David had in effect saved Saul's life and kingdom.

Here the commentators reach for the gravity of the phrase 'innocent blood.' To shed it is a crime that stains not only the shedder but, as the older writers feared, his house after him. Jonathan will not entail such guilt upon his family, and so he pleads not merely for David's safety but against Saul's own soul. The witnesses see in the plea a foreshadowing of every intercession made for the innocent before an unjust power: the appeal to what the accused has done, to what he has not done, and to the conscience of the judge who once rejoiced in the very man he now seeks to destroy. Right words, Henry concludes, are forcible; and for a moment they prevail.
6:
Saul yields, and swears David shall not die — but the witnesses divide over how much to trust the oath. The Reformed Gill reads the change as a genuine, if shallow, work upon Saul's mind: he not only heard Jonathan out but was affected and wrought upon by his words, convicted for the present that he was wrong to seek David's life, the LORD inclining his heart to listen. The grammatical-historical Ellicott agrees that the moving eloquence of Jonathan touched Saul's heart, and for a brief space something of the old noble spirit influenced the king, so that he swore he would not attempt David's life. Clarke adds the practical consequence: in keeping with this oath Saul must have issued orders contrary to those he had given the day before.

The great question the expositors weigh is whether the oath was sincere. The wesleyan Wesley takes it at face value: 'without all doubt he intended what he said, feeling a real change in himself for the present,' and he borrows Henry's own words — 'God inclined the heart of Saul to hearken to the voice of Jonathan.' The puritan Matthew Henry, however, is more troubled. Saul at other times swore rashly and profanely, making a by-word of the oath; and those who can so jest with an oath as to prostitute it to a trifle may not scruple, to serve a turn, to prostitute it to a lie. Some, Henry reports, even suspect Saul swore this with a malicious design to bring David within reach again — though, bad as Saul was, Henry can 'scarcely think so ill of him.'

So the witnesses reach a shared verdict beneath their difference of tone. Whether or not the oath was hollow from the first, all agree with Henry that Saul spoke as he thought for the present, but 'the convictions soon wore off and his corruptions prevailed and triumphed over them.' The moment displays both the reach and the limit of conviction without grace: the LORD can bend even a murderer's heart to swear by his Maker that the innocent shall live, and yet a heart unrenewed will not hold the oath a week. Right words are forcible, but they do not of themselves make a new man.
7:
Reconciliation is made, and David returns to court — but every witness hears the ominous undertone. The Reformed Gill notes the simple mechanics: Jonathan called David out of his lurking place in the field after Saul had gone home, and showed him all that had passed. Clarke adds that, the king having sworn David should not be slain, David now resumes his place in the palace of Saul, the very post he had quitted on the day appointed for his assassination. The scene appears, for a moment, to be a full restoration: David 'was in his presence, as in times past.'

Yet the grammatical-historical commentators will not let the reader rest in that calm. Keil and Delitzsch observe flatly that 'this reconciliation, unfortunately, did not last long,' and pass at once to the fresh war that would rekindle Saul's fury. Ellicott catches the same foreboding in the phrase itself: the old life went on as before, and David seemingly was received on terms of intimacy and affection by the king, but a new cause was soon supplied which again lit up the slumbering fires of jealousy in Saul's heart — the next verse tells of a successful campaign in which, as usual, David was the hero.

The witnesses thus make of this brief peace a study in the fragility of a reconciliation that reaches the conduct but not the heart. Matthew Henry gives the pastoral turn: Jonathan brought David to Saul hoping the storm was now over, and that he might be the instrument to keep his father always in this good mind. It was a good hope, honestly held, and it was disappointed — not because Jonathan's peacemaking was false, but because the peace rested on a man whose corruptions had only been checked, not cured. The expositors read the phrase 'as in times past' with a kind of sorrow: the old intimacy is restored to the letter, while beneath it the old envy waits for its occasion. Where enmity is rooted in the unrenewed heart, the commentators warn, even sworn reconciliation is but a truce, and the next victory of the hated one will prove how little it healed.
8:
War returns, David conquers again, and the witnesses set his faithfulness against Saul's revived hatred. The Reformed Gill keeps the historical scale modest: neither side sent out its whole force, only parties or detachments, with skirmishes between them. But the puritan Matthew Henry draws the moral portrait in bold strokes. David continues his good services to king and country, though Saul had requited him evil for good and his very usefulness was the thing Saul envied. Those who are ill paid for doing good, Henry urges, must not therefore be weary of well-doing, remembering what a bountiful benefactor our heavenly Father is even to the froward and unthankful. The wesleyan Wesley echoes the point almost word for word: David continues his good service, though it was ill requited.

The commentators dwell on David's magnanimity. He is as bold as ever in using his sword for his country, charging the Philistines and coming off victorious; and, Henry adds, he will presently be as cheerful as ever in using his harp for the prince who last time hurled a javelin at him (1 Sam. 18:10). He might have pleaded that harp-playing was now a service beneath him, or too dangerous to repeat; but a humble man thinks nothing below him by which he may do good, and David had learned to render good for evil and to trust God with his safety in the way of his duty. Henry hears in this the very heart that later wrote of weeping and fasting when his enemy was sick (Ps. 35:13–14).

Against this the witnesses set Saul's implacable malice, and they trace its spiritual mechanics. The fresh honours David won, instead of extinguishing Saul's ill-will, revived his envy and exasperated him the more; and when Saul indulged this wicked passion, no marvel that the evil spirit came upon him. Henry presses the sober lesson from Eph. 4:26–27: when we let the sun go down upon our wrath we give place to the devil, making room for him and inviting him in. So incurable, Gill and Henry agree, is the enmity of the serpent against the seed of the woman, and so deceitful and desperately wicked is the heart of man without the grace of God (Jer. 17:9).
9:
The javelin flies again, and the witnesses fasten on the hardest phrase in the chapter: 'the evil spirit from the LORD.' The Reformed Gill keeps close to the surface — Saul's melancholy and frantic disorder returned upon David's victories, and he grew envious, jealous, spiteful, and malicious. The wesleyan Clarke and Wesley read it as envy re-armed: David's successes revived Saul's jealousy, producing a distraction of mind that was exacerbated by diabolic influence, the devil watching his opportunity as before.

The grammatical-historical witnesses probe the theology more deeply. Keil and Delitzsch insist the phrase 'Ruach Jehovah' describes the attack of madness according to its higher cause, as inflicted by Jehovah in contrast with the 'Ruach Elohim' of the earlier chapters (1 Sam. 16:14–15); the growth of Saul's melancholy was a sign of the hardness of heart to which Jehovah had given him up on account of his impenitence. Ellicott confronts the difficulty head on: it is a hard saying, and 'no human expositor has ever yet been able fully to explain it.' He notes that the LXX, offended at 'evil spirit of Jehovah,' simply cuts the knot by leaving out 'Jehovah.' Refusing that evasion, Ellicott lays down two cautious propositions: that evil spirits exist, probably beings fallen through sin from their high estate; and that such spirits, still belonging to the Eternal even in their degradation, receive occasional permission to tempt and plague certain souls — as a trial of faith, as with Job (Job 1:6; 2:1–7), or as a punishment for the soul's desertion of God, as with Saul. He points also to the lying spirit before Ramoth-Gilead (1 Kings 22:19–22).

Ellicott lets the sorrow of the scene sound through Browning's poem 'Saul': the harp that once roused the king 'into life again' now fails, and this time, seizing the tall spear ever by his side, he hurls it with deadly intent at the sorrow-stricken, loving face of the very man who played to soothe him. Beneath their differences — Gill's plain melancholy, K&D's judicial hardening, Ellicott's permitted tormentor — the witnesses agree on what they will not pretend to solve: that God in righteous judgment may withdraw his restraining grace and give a man over to the spirit he has chosen, so that the sufferer's own indulged sin becomes his torment.
10:
Saul's spear buries itself in the wall, and David escapes into the night. The Reformed Gill notes that Saul sought to smite David even to the wall with his javelin, to strike it through him and pin him fast, as he had attempted before. The grammatical-historical Ellicott identifies the weapon precisely: 'the javelin' is the great spear which in so many scenes of First Samuel is shown in Saul's hand or by his side — the ready emblem of a king whose court had become an armed ambush. The wesleyan Clarke reads David's survival in very human terms: he found he could not trust Saul and was therefore continually on his watch, and his agility of body was the means of his preservation at this time.

Keil and Delitzsch render the moment vividly: David slipped away out of Saul's presence, so that the king hurled the javelin into the wall, whereupon David fled and escaped that night. The puritan Matthew Henry reads the same scene as a contest of unseen powers. So strong was the devil in Saul, so strong his own rage and passion, that he ran at David violently enough to nail him to the wall; perhaps, Henry suggests, he even flattered himself that if he killed David in such a fit he would be excusable before God and men as not in his right mind, and the death might be imputed to his distraction. But God cannot be deceived by pretences, whatever men may be.

The witnesses thus hold two truths in a single frame. On one side is the frightening freedom of a heart given over to wrath — Clarke's watchful fugitive, Ellicott's ever-present spear, Henry's man so possessed by passion that he would murder in his own dining-hall. On the other is the quiet, insistent care of God. Saul missed his blow; David was too quick for him; and by a kind providence he escaped that night. Henry hears in this preservation one of the many to which David would afterward point in the Psalms when he called God his shield and buckler, his rock and fortress, who delivered his soul from death. The javelin fixed in the wall becomes, for the expositors, a monument to the plain lesson that the wrath of man cannot outrun the guardianship of God.
11:
The hunt now follows David home, and the witnesses watch the trap close around his house. The Reformed Gill places the scene, probably at Gibeah where Saul lived, and notes that the king, supposing David had gone straight home, sent messengers to lie in wait. The wesleyan Clarke explains the timing of the order to slay him 'in the morning': by daylight the assassins could distinguish David from Michal his wife, avoiding danger to her in a night attack, and Saul could color the murder as the lawful seizing of a traitor, justifiable even in fullest daylight.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the night as the very setting of the fifty-ninth Psalm. David might well complain that his enemies were bloody men, as he does in the psalm penned on this occasion, when Saul 'sent, and they watched the house to kill him' (Ps. 59); there he laments that swords were in their lips, and that they made a noise like a dog going about the city. Henry marvels at the divine irony in David's rescue: Michal, whom Saul had given to David to be a snare to him, proves instead his protector and helper — 'often is the devil out-shot with his own bow.' She perceived the soldiers about the house as they settled for the night, suspected her father's design, and bestirred herself for her husband's safety.

The commentators are struck that David, so skilled in fighting, must now be saved by flight. Henry observes that David was better versed in the art of fighting than of flying; had it been lawful, he could easily have cleared his house by dint of sword. Instead he submits to be let down and to flee, and in that very peril his faith stands fixed on God. Whereas the plot was to slay him in the morning, David sings in the psalm with the greatest assurance, 'I will sing aloud of thy mercy in the morning.' So the witnesses find in this first night of David's long exile the pattern of the persecuted saint: hemmed in by bloody men and lying-in-wait, yet composed in mind, strong in faith, and rescued by a providence that turns the enemy's own snare into an instrument of deliverance.
12:
Michal lets David down through the window, and the witnesses at once reach for the great parallels of Scripture. The Reformed Gill sets the deed beside two famous escapes: Michal let David down 'in like manner as Rahab let down the spies from her house in Jericho' (Josh. 2:15), and as the disciples let the apostle Paul down the wall at Damascus to save him from the Jews (Acts 9:25; 2 Cor. 11:33). The wesleyan Clarke supplies the practical geography: Saul's messengers guarded the gate, so Michal let David down, probably from a back window, where there was neither entrance nor issue, and the liers-in-wait were easily eluded.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott gives the moment its human pathos. The princess, his wife, knew well her father's character; conscious now that the veil of his dark design was publicly lifted and that there was no hope for her husband save in instant flight, she let David down through the window because the king's guards were watching the door. 'With this desperate flight,' Ellicott writes, 'began those long weary wanderings, those perpetual risks of his life, which went on until the death of King Saul released David from his deadly enemy.' Keil and Delitzsch record the bare fact with equal weight: 'he went, and fled, and escaped' — three verbs that carry the whole change in David's fortunes.

The witnesses see in the window a turning-point as much as a rescue. Until this night David had been the king's champion, son-in-law, and captain; from it he becomes the hunted man of the wilderness years. Yet the expositors refuse to read the escape as mere flight. By placing it in the company of Rahab and Paul, Gill makes it an instance of the recurring providence by which God preserves his chosen through the courage of an unlikely deliverer and the open window of an ordinary house. The mighty man of valour is saved not by his sword but by a wife's quick love and a rope in the dark — a reminder, the commentators note, that the LORD who had anointed David to a throne would bring him to it by the way of weakness and dependence, not by the strength of his own arm.
13:
Michal's stratagem with the image draws the witnesses into a small puzzle of household religion. The word is 'teraphim,' and the Reformed Gill is troubled by its presence in David's house: if these were the same sort of thing Rachel stole from Laban, they seem akin to the household gods of the heathen, privately kept by Michal, for had David known of them he would not have suffered them there. Gill records the rabbinic guesses — Aben Ezra thought them talismans made under a constellation to draw down heavenly influences and foretell the future — but insists that to consult such images was far from David, who knew Saul's intent without them. The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the philology: teraphim is a plural form used as a singular, its Latin cousin 'penates' likewise found only in the plural; these idols, he says, were probably a remnant of the idolatry originally brought by some of Abraham's family from their Chaldaean home, and varied in size from the small image Rachel hid under the camel-saddle (Gen. 31:34) to the life-size figure Michal here used.

On the second detail the commentators divide. The wesleyan Clarke takes the image to be something in human form, since it was meant to represent a man lying sick in bed, and reads the 'pillow of goats' hair' as the appearance of a sick man's head muffled up, the goats' hair perhaps standing in for David's own hair. Wesley agrees that the goats' hair was set about the head and face of the image, the better to counterfeit David's hair in the dim, glimmering light of a sick-room. Ellicott reports the rival reading: some render the phrase 'a goat's-skin about its head,' following the Syriac and Vulgate, while the LXX read not 'skin' but 'liver,' so that Josephus explained Michal as placing a still-palpitating goat's liver in the bed to mimic a breathing sleeper.

Beneath the antiquarian debate the witnesses agree on Gill's suggestion that Michal, like Rachel, likely kept the teraphim secretly, perhaps because of her barrenness (Gen. 31:19). And they agree on the deeper irony the scene exposes: that even in the house of the LORD's anointed, and in the very act of a brave rescue, the old Chaldaean idolatry lingered. The image that saved David's life was itself a witness to how stubbornly the heart of Israel clung to its forbidden gods — a lifeless thing pressed into the service of the living God's providence, yet no less a reproach for that.
14:
The messengers come to seize David, and Michal turns them back with three words: 'He is sick.' The Reformed Gill supplies the setting — either these were the same men who had inquired at first light, or, staying longer than Saul expected, Saul feared them negligent or corrupted and sent others. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch follow the deception forward to Michal's later self-defence: when charged with sending away the king's enemy, she replies that David had said to her, 'Let me go; why should I kill thee?' — intimating that she did not mean to help him escape, but was forced to yield to a threat on her life. 'This prevarication,' K&D observe drily, 'she seems to have considered perfectly justifiable.'

Here the moral question the whole passage raises comes plainly into view, and the witnesses do not flinch from it. The delay Michal buys is real mercy — every hour keeps the assassins from the pursuit and lets David put distance between himself and Gibeah. Yet the means is a lie, and Matthew Henry will not gloss it: Michal can by no means be justified in telling a falsehood and covering it thus with a cheat, for 'God's truth needed not her lie.' Her aim, to keep Saul in suspense so that David might secure himself, was good; but the deceit that served it was not thereby made lawful.

The commentators thus hold the daughter of Saul in a careful double light. She is brave, loyal, and quick, cleaving to her husband against her own father and risking his wrath; and in that she is to be honoured. But her courage is not matched by candour, and the expositors mark the difference between her stratagem and the open, truthful pleading by which Jonathan had earlier stood between David and death. Where Jonathan spoke good of David to Saul's face and hazarded himself in the truth, Michal shields David with a fabricated sickness and a fabricated threat. The witnesses draw the sober lesson that a right end does not sanctify a false means, and that the providence of God, who was even then guarding David by a hundred lawful turns, stood in no need of a lie to accomplish his purpose.
15:
Saul's answer to Michal's report exposes the depth of his cruelty. The Reformed Gill catches the cold intent of the second embassy: Saul sent the messengers again 'to see David' — not to visit him or kindly inquire after his health, but to look upon his person, to learn whether he was truly sick and whether he was even there, for the king now suspected some deceit was used, since his men had taken Michal's word and never actually set eyes on David. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the chilling command in full: 'Bring him up to me in the bed, that I may slay him.'

The puritan Matthew Henry presses the horror of the order home. It was base and barbarous, he says, thus to triumph over a sick man, and to vow the death of one who for aught Saul knew was already dying by the hand of nature. So earnestly did the king thirst after David's blood, and so greedy was his revenge, that he could not be content to see David dead — unless he himself were the death of him. Henry sets the command against Saul's own words a little earlier, 'Let not my hand be upon him,' and marks how far the man has fallen: 'when men lay the reins on the neck of their passions they grow more and more outrageous.'

The witnesses find in the sick-bed order a portrait of malice that will be balked by nothing. A night's failure has not cooled Saul but inflamed him; a report of illness that might have moved pity in another only sharpens his resolve to strike the helpless. The expositors note the grim resolve to have David 'in the bed,' as if the very posture of weakness that should have disarmed the pursuer instead invited the blow. Yet the reader who knows the empty bed awaits these messengers sees also the restraining hand of God: the more furiously Saul commands, the more plainly the sequel will show that his reach cannot overtake the man Heaven has hidden. The verse stands, for the commentators, as a study in the self-consuming rage of the persecutor, whose passion drives him to a cruelty that is at once monstrous and, before the providence of God, entirely vain.
16:
The messengers enter, and the trick is laid bare. The Reformed Gill takes the reader with them into the room where David was supposed to lie, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch report what they found: 'behold, there was an image in the bed, with a pillow of goats' hair for his bolster.' The counterfeit sleeper that had turned back the first embassy now lies exposed, and Michal's stratagem, which had bought a night, can buy no more.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the discovery with a certain calm. By this time, he judges, David was safe, and therefore Michal was not much concerned at the discovery: the whole aim of the deceit had been to keep Saul in suspense long enough for David to get clear, and that end was now gained. The unmasking of the image, far from a defeat, marks the success of the delay — the hunters have their proof of the trick precisely when their quarry is already beyond their reach.

K&D carry the narrative straight on to its next stage, and the witnesses follow the thread gladly. David fled, and escaped, and came to Samuel at Ramah, and told him all that Saul had done to him. Here the commentators lift their eyes from the empty bed to the destination of the flight. The image in the bed had done its work; the man it screened was already on the road to the one place in Israel where he might find both counsel and communion — the prophet who had anointed him. So the verse that seems only to record a foiled search in fact turns the story: the last of David's court-rescues gives way to the first of his refuges in the fellowship of the LORD's servants. The witnesses note the quiet providence in the sequence. Every device that saved David that night — the window, the rope, the idol under the coverlet — was but the outer shell of a deliverance whose true author was the God who now drew his anointed to Samuel's side, that David might be strengthened by the man of God for the long trial still before him.
17:
Saul rounds on his daughter, and Michal's excuse becomes the last, most troubling turn of the rescue. The puritan Matthew Henry feels the injustice of the king's reproach first: what a base spirit was Saul, to expect that because Michal was his daughter she must therefore betray her own husband to him unjustly. Ought she not, Henry asks, to forsake and forget her father's house to cleave to her husband? Those who will themselves be held by no bonds of reason or religion are quick to think others should as easily break them. Yet Henry turns at once to Michal's answer with real sorrow, for she saves herself by slandering David: she tells Saul, 'He said, Let me go; why should I kill thee?' — representing the gentle David as a brutal and imperious husband who had threatened her life.

The wesleyan Clarke is gentler with her. The words mean, in effect, 'If thou do not let me go, I will kill thee,' and Michal said this to excuse herself to her father; as a wife she could do no less than favour her husband's escape, being perfectly satisfied there was no guilt in him. Clarke, like Henry, connects the night to the fifty-ninth Psalm, 'Deliver me from mine enemies,' which David may well have written on this very occasion (Ps. 59).

So the witnesses divide over Michal even as they honour her courage. Henry judges her falsehood the more sharply because it did not even have the plea of necessity: her earlier lie about David's sickness had gained the needed time, but this fresh invention needlessly blackened her innocent husband and might have confirmed Saul in his rage against him. It manifests, Henry concludes, that she was not moved by the same spirit of piety that had dictated Jonathan's language to Saul. The contrast the whole chapter has been drawing now stands complete: two of Saul's children shelter David, but Jonathan shelters him in the truth and Michal in a lie. And over both, Henry hears the deeper note — David suffered wrongfully from friends as well as foes, and so, in his day, did the Son of David.
18:
David's flight now finds its goal, and the witnesses dwell on where the fugitive chose to run. The Reformed Gill notes simply that David fled and escaped from his own house and the messengers of Saul; but the puritan Matthew Henry makes the destination the whole point. Having got away in the night, David did not run to Bethlehem to his relations, nor to any of the cities that had cried him up, to raise an interest for his own preservation; he ran straight to Samuel and told him all that Saul had done. In flying to Samuel, Henry says, David made God his refuge, trusting in the shadow of his wings; where else can a good man think himself safe?

Henry gives three reasons the witnesses gladly follow. First, Samuel was the man who had assured David of the crown, and now, when David's faith in that promise was beginning to fail and he was ready to say in his haste, 'All men are liars' (Ps. 116:11), whither should he go but to the prophet for such encouragements as would support his faith? Second, Samuel as a prophet was best able to advise him what to do in this day of distress; the night before David had lifted up his prayer to God, and now he takes the first opportunity to receive direction, for if we expect answers of peace to our prayers we must have our ears open to God's word. Third, with Samuel was a college of prophets whose praises would be the greatest relief to his troubled spirit.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the double motive: David came to Samuel partly to seek further counsel from the prophet who had anointed him, and partly to strengthen himself, by intercourse with the man of God, for the troubles that still awaited him; and so he went with Samuel and dwelt at Naioth. The witnesses close the verse on Henry's memorable contrast: David 'met with little rest or satisfaction in Saul's court, and therefore went to seek it in Samuel's church.' It is the lesson the expositors press upon every reader — that what little pleasure is to be had in this world belongs to those who live a life of communion with God, and that to this fellowship the believer must retire in the time of trouble (Ps. 27:4–6).
19:
Word of David's whereabouts reaches Saul, and the witnesses pause to explain the strange place named Naioth. The Reformed Gill notes that it was told Saul by some officious persons who had seen David at Ramah and observed him go with Samuel to Naioth — the informers who dog the steps of the hunted in every age. The grammatical-historical Ellicott clears up what Naioth was: not a town, but, as the name denotes, a cluster of dwellings or abodes, from a verb meaning to rest or abide. Samuel had his own house in Ramah, and these dwellings, where his prophetic schools were established, stood in the immediate neighbourhood — hence 'Naioth in Ramah.' The Chaldee Targum, Ellicott adds, renders the name 'house of learning.'

Keil and Delitzsch handle the finer points of the Hebrew with characteristic care. When Saul learned where the place was, he sent messengers to fetch David; and the text's striking singular verb — 'and he saw' the company of prophets — K&D take not as a copyist's slip but as the original reading, understood either of the leader of the messengers or of the whole company regarded as one body. They note too that the rare word rendered 'company' or 'assembly' occurs only here, arising, the older rabbis thought, by simple inversion from the ordinary word for a congregation.

The witnesses see in this quiet verse the setting for the wonder about to unfold. David has fled from a court bristling with spears to a settlement given over to prayer, praise, and the study of God's word under the aged prophet's oversight. It is, in Ellicott's phrase, a house of learning — a company of men gathered to abide together in the things of God. Into this haven of worship Saul's messengers are now dispatched with murderous intent, and the expositors let the contrast stand for the reader to feel: the armed emissaries of a jealous king bearing down upon a school of the prophets. The very geography of the verse prepares the lesson the chapter will close upon, that there is a shelter in the assemblies of the faithful which the malice of the mighty cannot violate, because the Spirit of God keeps the door.
20:
Saul's first assassins reach the school of the prophets and are overwhelmed by the Spirit. The Reformed Gill marks the impiety of the errand — so bent was Saul on David's destruction that he sent to take him even in that sacred place. But the wesleyan Clarke states the reversal plainly: the company of prophets were employed in their religious exercises, Samuel appointed over them as head or president of the school, and when the Spirit of God came upon the messengers they partook of the same influence and joined in the same exercise, and thus were prevented from seizing David. The wesleyan Wesley explains Samuel's oversight: he stood over the prophets to instruct and direct them, to encourage the gift and detect any imposture, for even those who prophesied by divine inspiration needed to prepare beforehand and improve it afterward; and the messengers prophesied 'being inspired by God as Balaam was,' so caught up in ecstasy that their minds were wholly taken off their design of seizing David.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott insists the event does not belong to ordinary natural experience, and the only possible explanation lies in the words immediately before it: 'The Spirit of God was upon these messengers of Saul.' He borrows Ewald's vivid picture: those who started with the most hostile intentions suddenly stood still, spell-bound by the music and solemn dance of the devotees; then, drawn more and more powerfully into the charmed circle, they broke into similar words and gestures, and, flinging away their upper garments, joined the dance and the song until, sinking into ecstatic quivering, they utterly forgot the hostile spirit in which they had come.

The witnesses agree that this is no mere psychological contagion but a work of God to shield his anointed. Instead of seizing David, the seizers were themselves seized. The expositors delight in the divine economy: God secures David without slaying a single messenger, as he had once made Balaam bless where he was hired to curse; He puts honour upon the communion of saints, showing how He can strike an awe upon the worst of men by the tokens of His presence in the assembly of the faithful (1 Cor. 14:24–25); and He magnifies His power over the spirits of men, that He who made the heart and tongue can manage both to serve His own purposes.
21:
Twice more Saul sends, and twice more his men prophesy instead of seizing. The Reformed Gill pictures the king's mounting bafflement: it was told Saul that the messengers he had sent, instead of laying hold on David, were themselves prophesying, or at least attending to services of a wholly different nature than the errand for which they were dispatched. A second company is sent, and they prophesy likewise; a third, and they prophesy also. The repetition is the point — the more force Saul multiplies against the school of the prophets, the more plainly the Spirit of God turns his instruments back upon themselves.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott lifts the incident into the worship of the wider church. He calls attention, with Bishop Wordsworth, to the fact that this portion of Scripture, from the eighteenth verse to the end of the chapter, which relates the descent of the Spirit upon Saul's messengers and even upon the king himself, was appointed by the church to be read on Whitsun Tuesday, in order to show the existence and working of the Holy Spirit before the times of the Gospel, and the freedom and power of His divine agency. Ellicott sets beside it the companion reading for Whitsun Monday, the history of Eldad and Medad, upon whom the Spirit rested in the camp so that they prophesied (Num. 11:26–31).

The witnesses read the threefold failure as a threefold sermon. Each fresh embassy is a fresh proof that Saul is fighting not against David but against God, and cannot prevail. The expositors note how patiently the warnings are multiplied: three times the king is shown, in the return of his own soldiers turned worshippers, that Heaven has drawn a line around his anointed which no armed party may cross. And they hear in the whole scene the anticipation of Pentecost that the church's ancient lectionary rightly discerned — that the same Spirit who would one day be poured out upon all flesh was already free and sovereign in the days of Samuel, able to arrest the malice of men by laying His hand upon their very tongues and turning their errand of murder into an act of praise.
22:
His messengers failing him, Saul at last goes himself, and the witnesses trace the king's relentless pursuit to the well of Sechu. The Reformed Gill notes that, his men not returning as he sent one company after another to take David, Saul at length set out in person from Gibeah to Ramah. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch follow him to the landmark named in the text: Saul came to the great well — or great pit — that is in Sechu, a place near Ramah with which we are otherwise unacquainted, and there inquired, 'Where are Samuel and David?' Being told, 'Behold, they be at Naioth in Ramah,' he turned his steps thither.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the king's personal coming as the measure of his frenzy. So impatient was Saul in his thirst after David's blood, so restless to compass his design, that though baffled by one providence after another he could not perceive that David was under the special protection of Heaven. It was beneath the dignity of a king to go himself on such an errand; but persecutors, Henry observes, will stoop to anything and stick at nothing to gratify their malice, laying aside all public business to hunt one man. The very grandeur of the pursuer only sharpens the folly of the pursuit.

The witnesses see the well of Sechu as the threshold of Saul's undoing. He pauses there to ask the way, as a man sure of his prey; he does not know that the next steps will strip him of his royal purpose as surely as they will presently strip him of his royal robes. The expositors mark the irony that God lets Saul come all the way — lets him gather his intelligence and set his face toward Naioth — only to overrule the whole design at the door of the prophets. Where the king imagines himself closing at last upon David, he is in truth walking into the hand of the God he is fighting, and the great well in Sechu becomes the last dry stage before the Spirit overtakes him.
23:
On the road to Naioth the pursuer is overtaken, and the witnesses mark the moment the Spirit falls on Saul himself. The Reformed Gill follows him plainly from the well toward the place, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record that the Spirit of God came upon Saul also, so that he went on and prophesied, all the way, until he came to Naioth in Ramah. The wesleyan Clarke notes where the seizure began: the divine Spirit seemed to grasp him at the well of Sechu, and from that point he went on prophesying — praying, singing praises, and the like — until he reached the prophets' dwellings.

The wesleyan Wesley draws out the significant difference from what had gone before. The Spirit came upon Saul in the way, whereas it had not come upon his messengers until they arrived at the place. By this, Wesley says, God would convince Saul of the utter vanity of his designs against David, and show him that in these designs he was fighting against God Himself. The king who had sent three parties to seize a man now finds himself seized long before he can reach him, disarmed of his purpose by the very hand he had set himself against.

The witnesses read this as the climax of the chapter's long demonstration of divine protection. Henry had observed how, throughout, God delivered David not by slaying the messengers, nor by fire from heaven, but 'by turning the lions for the present into lambs'; and now the fiercest lion of all is turned. That so bad a man as Saul should be caught up into prophecy is, the expositors admit, a wonder — but it is exactly the wonder God intends, that even the persecutor's mouth should be made to confess, in praise and prophecy, the God whose anointed he came to kill. The nearer Saul draws to David, the more completely the Spirit masters him, until the whole journey of murder becomes an involuntary act of worship, and every step toward the prey a step deeper into the power of the LORD. What began at Gibeah as a march of settled murder ends, before ever it reaches its object, as a helpless confession of the God who reigns over the hearts of kings.
24:
The chapter ends with the astonishing sight of Saul stripped and prostrate among the prophets, and the witnesses gather to read its meaning. The Reformed Gill and the wesleyan Clarke agree on the plain sense of 'he stripped off his clothes': not that Saul lay wholly bare, but that he threw off his royal robes and warlike habiliments, retaining only his tunic, and — as the rabbis suggest — put on something of the habit of the sons of the prophets. Clarke sends the reader back to Saul's first ecstasy after his anointing (1 Sam. 10:6), and Keil and Delitzsch confirm from Scripture's usage that 'naked' often means only that the upper garment is laid aside (Isa. 20:2; John 21:7). The wesleyan Wesley adds that Saul 'fell down upon the earth,' his mind in such an ecstasy that he had not the use of his senses, and lay so all that day and night — God keeping him as it were in chains until David was got out of his reach, and making Saul 'in some sort a prophet, that he might make David a king.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the harder question: what did this second outpouring mean for a faithless king? He recalls that after his anointing Saul had been 'changed into another man' (1 Sam. 10:10), and asks whether it is not better to understand that once more the pitiful Spirit pleaded with the man the LORD had chosen — a pleading soon forgotten when the strange excitement passed, as, Ellicott sighs, is the matter of every day. The old proverb revives, 'Is Saul also among the prophets?' (1 Sam. 10:12), for men found something strange in one so self-willed receiving the coveted gift.

Here the one voice of the ancient church in this chapter speaks the closing word. Ellicott gathers up the latin father Augustine, who preached on this very scene: 'Many are the gifts of God which are possessed by evil men... The gift of prophecy is a great gift, but it was possessed by Saul — an evil king who prophesied at the very time he was persecuting holy David. Let not men boast if they have God's gifts; those gifts will profit them nothing without charity' (1 Cor. 13:1–2). So the witnesses close united: the Spirit that stripped and silenced Saul preserved David and confounded the persecutor, yet the same Spirit's gift, held without love and without obedience, left Saul only more accountable — a solemn proof that a man may prophesy among the prophets and still be lost.
